data_source_summary: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN (June 2008, updated 2023); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; State Forest Portal (ecotourism statistics)

Q1.src: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6

Q2.v: Forest Department, Chhattisgarh (co-implemented with Tourism Department and Community Committees)
Q2.src: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN (updated 2023) — Implementation clause: Forest Department + Tourism Department + Community committees

Q3.v: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN (June 1 2008, updated 2023); Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25; Outcome Budget 2025-26
Q3.src: programs.md source block ANNOUNCEMENT, BUDGET, CAG, SURVEY, OUTCOME

Q4.r: The primary resource is Chhattisgarh's protected-area forest estate — 28 sites comprising 3 National Parks, 8 Wildlife Sanctuaries, and 17 Reserved Forests. These are state/public land. However, Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN (updated 2023) formally notifies these areas for community-benefit ecotourism: mandating 60% of tourism revenue to local communities, local employment priority for guides/staff, and community committees as co-implementers. This formal notification for community use, combined with active community-governance structures, classifies the resource as A1 (State/public land formally notified for community use) rather than A2 (state-revenue extraction). The scheme's design purpose is community livelihood from ecological commons, not state revenue maximisation.
Q4.src: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN (updated 2023) — Protected areas clause, Community benefit clause (60% revenue), Employment clause (local priority), Implementation clause

Q5.v: 28 protected areas across Chhattisgarh state: 3 National Parks (including Guru Ghasi-Das, Kanger Valley), 8 Wildlife Sanctuaries (including Udanti-Sitanadi), 17 Reserved Forests with operational ecotourism. Trail network: 850 km across these areas. Biodiversity: 458 bird species, 125 mammal species including tiger, gaur, bison. 45 eco-resorts (850 rooms). Boundaries legally defined under Wildlife Protection Act (NPs and WLSs) and Indian Forest Act (RFs) — each site individually notified with survey-demarcated boundaries in state forest records.
Q5.src: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN updated 2023 — Protected areas clause; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — trail map survey, license records; Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25

Q6.r: All 28 protected areas have legally demarcated boundaries under Wildlife Protection Act 1972 (National Parks and Sanctuaries) and Indian Forest Act 1927 (Reserved Forests). CAG Report 2024-25 confirms trail map survey covering 850 km across all sites. Ecotourism notification specifies 28 named sites. Boundaries exist in official forest survey records, WPA gazettes, and state forest portal directory.
Q6.src: Wildlife Protection Act 1972 — gazette notifications for NPs and WLSs; Indian Forest Act 1927 — RF notifications; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — trail map survey; State Forest Portal — protected area directory

Q7.r: Community access right is statutory and legally enforceable on two grounds: (1) Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN (updated 2023) is a statutory instrument mandating that 60% of ecotourism revenue accrues to local communities and establishing employment rights (local employment priority for 5,200 guide/staff positions) — these are enforceable scheme entitlements. (2) In tribal-inhabited protected areas (applicable to several of the 28 sites in Chhattisgarh), communities hold rights under Forest Rights Act 2006 (community forest rights) and PESA 1996 (Gram Sabha powers over village commons in scheduled areas). Score = 2.
Q7.src: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN updated 2023 — Community benefit clause, Employment clause; Forest Rights Act 2006; PESA 1996; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — licensing database (5,200 guides confirmed)

Q8.r: No active diversion order, mining lease, land-use change notification, SEZ notification, or compulsory acquisition proceeding exists on the 28 protected areas during the scheme period. All sites are legally protected under Wildlife Protection Act 1972 (NPs and WLSs) and Indian Forest Act 1927 (RFs), which bar such diversions without supreme court approval. CAG audit 2024-25 confirms all 28 sites are operational with no encumbrance. Budget 2025-26 approves ongoing expenditure, confirming no diversion process is active.
Q8.src: Wildlife Protection Act 1972 — Section 26A (prohibition on diversion without SC approval); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — 28 protected areas counted with operating licenses; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6

Q9.breakdown: Infrastructure (eco-resorts, trails, visitor facilities): ₹30 cr; Marketing: ₹20 cr; Community livelihood: ₹12 cr; Conservation: ₹3 cr. Actual spend: ₹62 cr (95% utilisation per CAG).
Q9.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department) — Ecotourism Development allocation; CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — ₹65 cr allocated, ₹62 cr spent

Q10.r: All four budget components constitute commons expenditure: (1) Infrastructure ₹30 cr — eco-resorts, trails (850 km), visitor centres are constructed on protected forest land (state/public commons) and serve the entire ecotourism enterprise including community guides/staff; outputs flow to the commons and community collectively. (2) Marketing ₹20 cr — promotes access to the protected-area ecological commons; benefits flow to community tourism employment collectively, not to any single private actor. (3) Community livelihood ₹12 cr — direct investment in community capacity (guide training, employment support); explicitly targets commons-dependent livelihoods. (4) Conservation ₹3 cr — direct investment in the ecological commons resource. No component flows exclusively to a private individual's private asset; all outputs are on or for the common protected-area resource.
Q10.src: Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 — budget breakdown; Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN updated 2023 — community benefit and employment clauses

Q11.r: No scheme expenditure flows exclusively to private assets. Eco-resorts (45 units) are on state protected-forest land with operator licences; the infrastructure investment (in ₹30 cr component) is on common land and serves community employment (5,200 guides/staff). No private land or private agricultural asset is subsidised. Q10 + Q11 = ₹65 cr = Q9.
Q11.src: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN updated 2023 — all infrastructure on protected state forest land; Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 — no private-asset line items

Q12.interp: PASS — θ₁ = 1.00, well above threshold of 0.50. All scheme expenditure is on protected-area commons resources and community livelihood on those commons.
Q12.src: Derived from Q9 and Q10 above

docs: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN (June 1 2008, updated 2023) — scheme establishment and budget mandate
Chhattisgarh State Budget 2025-26 Demand for Grants No. 6 (Forest Department) — ₹65 cr allocation with component breakdown
CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — ₹65 cr allocated, ₹62 cr spent (95%), revenue and fund transfer records verified
Chhattisgarh Economic Survey 2024-25 — employment, revenue, and tourism statistics

Q14.r: Benefits are partially excludable (score 1). Entry to protected areas requires payment of fee (₹200–500 per visitor); employment as guides/staff is limited to locally registered and licensed individuals (5,200 out of a broader community); revenue sharing (60%) accrues to defined local communities near protected areas rather than all citizens. However, exclusion is not absolute — ecosystem benefits (clean air, watershed, biodiversity conservation) are non-excludable for surrounding communities, and the locally-targeted employment/revenue sharing is broadly accessible to community members. Fee-based entry triggers the equity cross-flag: T2 audit must verify that the entry fee does not suppress marginalised beneficiary participation (T2.2, θ₃).
Q14.src: Official Notification No. 6-ECO/2008/CGN updated 2023 — Entry fees clause (₹200–500), Employment clause (5,200 local), Community benefit clause (60% revenue); CAG State Accounts Report 2024-25 — licensing database
